stem: What is the primary characteristic that defines a derivative contract?
option_a: It is a loan agreement between two financial institutions
option_b: Its value is derived from an underlying asset such as equity, commodity, or currency
option_c: It guarantees a fixed return irrespective of market conditions
option_d: It directly transfers ownership of an underlying asset immediately
explanation: A derivative is a contract whose value is derived from an underlying asset — which can be equity, commodity, currency, interest rate, etc. It does not transfer ownership immediately nor guarantee fixed returns.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Chapter: Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part I

stem: A company enters into a contract to purchase 1,000 kg of cotton at ₹150/kg three months from now, with actual delivery of cotton expected at settlement. This is best described as a derivative entered into for:
option_a: Upside betting on cotton prices
option_b: Speculative shorting of commodity prices
option_c: Cash settlement purposes
option_d: Doing a transaction on a future date with settlement by delivery
explanation: When a derivative is entered into for transacting on a future date with actual delivery of the underlying asset, it is a delivery-based contract — not a cash-settled speculative bet.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Chapter: Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part I

stem: An investor buys a derivative contract expecting the price of a stock to rise, with no intention of acquiring the stock — settlement will happen in cash. This is best classified as:
option_a: Upside betting on the price of the underlying asset
option_b: A currency swap for risk management
option_c: A delivery-based forward contract
option_d: A commodity hedge with physical settlement
explanation: When an investor takes a position expecting price rise with no intent of physical delivery and settlement happens in cash, this is upside betting on the underlying asset's price — a speculative derivative use.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Chapter: Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part I

stem: Which of the following statements about settlement of derivative contracts is most accurate?
option_a: Settlement method is determined solely by the type of underlying asset
option_b: Generally, cash settlement is the norm across derivative contracts regardless of purpose
option_c: All derivative contracts are settled by physical delivery of the underlying asset
option_d: Cash settlement is done only in speculative contracts, never in hedging contracts
explanation: Although derivative contracts can theoretically be settled by delivery or cash, in practice cash settlement is the general norm across all derivative contracts.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part I

stem: A trader has no existing position in any stock but enters a futures contract betting that Reliance's share price will fall. He plans to profit from the price decline and settle in cash. He is best classified as a:
option_a: Hedger
option_b: Arbitrageur
option_c: Market Maker
option_d: Speculator
explanation: A speculator has no existing exposure/risk but enters a derivative contract to earn profit by betting on price movement. Since this trader has no underlying position and is purely betting on a price fall, he is a speculator.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part I

stem: An exporter has a receivable of USD 1,00,000 due in 3 months and enters a currency forward contract to sell USD at today's rate. Which category of derivative market participant does this exporter represent?
option_a: Market Maker
option_b: Arbitrageur
option_c: Speculator
option_d: Hedger
explanation: A hedger already has an existing exposure (risk) — here the exporter is exposed to USD depreciation risk — and uses a derivative to eliminate that risk. This is the defining characteristic of a hedger.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part I

stem: Nifty futures are trading at ₹19,800 on NSE while simultaneously quoting at ₹19,820 on BSE. A participant buys on NSE and sells on BSE simultaneously, locking in a riskless ₹20 profit per unit with no prior market exposure. This participant is best described as:
option_a: A speculator taking a bullish view
option_b: An arbitrageur exploiting market mispricing
option_c: A market maker providing liquidity
option_d: A hedger eliminating index risk
explanation: An arbitrageur has no existing exposure but exploits mispricing across markets to earn riskless profit. The simultaneous buy-low-sell-high across exchanges with no directional bet is the hallmark of arbitrage.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part I

stem: A futures contract on USD/INR exchange rate would be classified under which category of derivatives based on underlying asset?
option_a: Equity Derivative
option_b: Currency Derivative
option_c: Other Derivative
option_d: Commodity Derivative
explanation: Derivatives are classified by underlying asset. A USD/INR futures contract has currency as the underlying asset, making it a Currency Derivative — also referred to as FOREX derivative in the AFM curriculum.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part I

stem: Which of the following best distinguishes a Futures contract from a Forward contract?
option_a: Futures contracts are traded on a stock exchange while forward contracts are OTC
option_b: Futures contracts do not create any obligation on either party
option_c: Futures contracts involve physical delivery while forward contracts are always cash settled
option_d: Forward contracts are traded on a stock exchange while futures are OTC
explanation: Forwards and futures are economically similar contracts but differ in trading venue. Forwards are Over-The-Counter (OTC) bilateral agreements while futures are standardised contracts traded on a stock exchange.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part II

stem: A forward contract was entered into between Company A and Company B for purchase of 500 shares at ₹300 each after 60 days. On maturity, the market price is ₹270. Company A wants to back out of the deal. Which statement is correct?
option_a: Company A can exit since the contract is loss-making for it
option_b: Only the seller (Company B) is obligated; the buyer has the option to exit
option_c: Both parties are obligated to fulfil the contract regardless of market price
option_d: Company B must release Company A since market price has fallen
explanation: Forwards and futures create obligation on both parties — each party must fulfil their promise in any case with no choice to exit. This distinguishes them from options where only the seller is obligated.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part II

stem: The current spot price of a share is ₹500. A 3-month futures contract exists on this share. The interest rate is 8% p.a. (simple interest) and no dividend is expected. What is the fair futures price?
explanation: Using simple interest: F = S × (1 + R×T) = 500 × (1 + 0.08 × 3/12) = 500 × 1.02 = ₹510. Since no compounding cue is given, simple interest applies by default.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part II, Cost of Carry Model

stem: A question states: 'Continuously compounded interest rate is 12% p.a.' The spot price is ₹800 and the futures contract matures in 6 months. No dividends expected. Which formula and result is correct?
explanation: When the question specifically mentions 'continuously compounded' interest rate, the formula F = S × e^(RT) must be applied. Here R = 0.12, T = 6/12 = 0.5, giving F = 800 × e^0.06.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part II, Cost of Carry Model

stem: The spot price of a share is ₹600. A 3-month futures contract is to be priced. The interest rate is 10% p.a. and the dividend yield on the share is 4% p.a. (continuously compounded). What is the correct formula to apply?
option_b: F = (600 - PV of Dividend) × e^(0.10 × 0.25)
explanation: When dividend yield rate (Y) is given instead of a fixed dividend amount, it is deducted from the interest rate R in the formula. For continuous compounding: F = S × e^((R-Y)×T) = 600 × e^(0.06 × 0.25).
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part II, Cost of Carry Model Note (ii)

stem: A 3-month futures contract on A Ltd.'s share is being priced. Spot price = ₹200, Face Value = ₹10, continuously compounded interest rate = 10% p.a. A 25% dividend on face value is expected after 2 months. Given: e^0.025 = 1.0253, e^(-0.0167) = 0.98344. What is the fair futures price?
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part II, Cost of Carry Model Example

stem: The spot price of a commodity is ₹450 and its futures price is ₹470. What is the Basis, and what market condition does this indicate?
option_a: Basis = +20; Backwardation market
option_b: Basis = -20; Backwardation market
option_c: Basis = -20; Contango market
option_d: Basis = +20; Contango market
explanation: Basis = S - F = 450 - 470 = -20. A negative basis (futures price > spot price) indicates a Contango market — the normal condition where futures trade at a premium to spot.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part II, Contango & Backwardation

stem: In a futures market, the spot price of a share is ₹380 and the 2-month futures contract is trading at ₹365. A student calculates Basis = S - F = +15 and concludes it is a Contango market. What is wrong with this conclusion?
option_a: Nothing is wrong — positive basis always indicates Contango
option_b: The Basis is correctly calculated but the market condition interpretation is reversed — positive Basis indicates Backwardation, not Contango
option_c: Basis cannot be calculated when futures price is below spot price
option_d: The Basis formula should be F - S, not S - F
explanation: The formula Basis = S - F is correct. A positive Basis (S > F) means futures are trading at a discount to spot, which is Backwardation. Contango is when Basis is negative (F > S). The student correctly computed the Basis but misidentified the market condition.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part II, Contango & Backwardation

stem: The fair futures price of Infosys is ₹1,480 per share. Lot size is 300 shares. A trader buys 5 lots. The actual quoted trading price in the market is not separately given. What is the total contract value?
explanation: When actual quoted trading price is not given, assume it equals the theoretical (fair) price. Price of 1 lot = ₹1,480 × 300 = ₹4,44,000. Contract value = ₹4,44,000 × 5 lots = ₹22,20,000.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part II, Price of 1 Future Contract

stem: A trader buys 4 lots of Nifty futures at ₹19,500 (lot size: 50 units). After 1 month the futures price rises to ₹19,800 and the trader squares off. What is the profit/loss on this position?
option_a: Loss of ₹60,000
option_b: Profit of ₹60,000
option_c: Profit of ₹6,000
option_d: Profit of ₹15,000
explanation: Profit per unit = 19,800 - 19,500 = ₹300. Total units = 4 lots × 50 = 200 units. Total profit = 300 × 200 = ₹60,000. Since the trader bought (long position) and price rose, it is a profit.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part II, Profit/Loss (Pay-off) on Future Contracts

stem: Mr. A sold 3 lots of Nifty futures at ₹18,500 (lot size: 50 units). On maturity, Nifty futures settle at ₹18,200. What is his pay-off?
option_a: Loss of ₹45,000
option_b: Gain of ₹45,000
option_c: Gain of ₹90,000
option_d: Loss of ₹90,000
explanation: Short position gains when price falls. Gain = 3 × 50 × (18,500 - 18,200) = 150 × 300 = ₹45,000. A seller of futures benefits from a price decline — this is downside betting.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part II, Pay-off on Futures

stem: On 1st January, Nifty futures trade at ₹20,000 (lot size: 50 units). Mr. A takes a long position on 2 lots. After 3 months, Nifty futures rise by 5%. What is Mr. A's gain?
explanation: Contract Value = 20,000 × 50 × 2 = ₹20,00,000. Gain = ₹20,00,000 × 5% = ₹1,00,000. Long position profits when price rises — alternatively: 2 × 50 × (21,000 - 20,000) = ₹1,00,000.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part II, Pay-off on Futures Example

stem: Mr. A is long on 2 lots of Nifty futures at ₹20,000 (lot size: 50). Nifty futures fall by 2% at maturity. Mr. B is short on the same 2 lots at the same price. Which of the following correctly describes the combined outcome?
option_a: Both break even since the contract is zero-sum
option_b: Mr. A loses ₹40,000; Mr. B gains ₹40,000
option_c: Both Mr. A and Mr. B make a loss of ₹40,000 each
option_d: Mr. A gains ₹40,000; Mr. B loses ₹40,000
explanation: Futures are a zero-sum game — one party's gain equals the other's loss. Contract Value = ₹20,00,000. 2% fall = ₹40,000 loss for the long (Mr. A) and ₹40,000 gain for the short (Mr. B). Long loses when price falls; short gains when price falls.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part II, Pay-off on Futures

stem: A trader holds a short position on 4 lots of a stock futures contract at ₹500 per share (lot size: 100). On maturity, the settlement price is ₹530. The trader argues that since he sold at ₹500 and bought back at ₹530, his loss should be ₹30 per share on only 1 lot. Identify the error in his reasoning.
option_a: Short positions always gain regardless of price movement
option_b: The loss of ₹30 per share applies to all 4 lots, giving total loss = 4 × 100 × ₹30 = ₹12,000
option_c: There is no error — loss is ₹30 per share on 1 lot only
option_d: The settlement price should be compared to fair value, not contracted price
explanation: Pay-off is calculated on total exposure = No. of lots × Lot size × Price difference. Loss = 4 × 100 × (530 - 500) = ₹12,000. The trader incorrectly limited the calculation to 1 lot.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part II, Pay-off on Futures

stem: Which of the following correctly describes the purpose of Initial Margin in futures contracts?
option_a: It is a penalty charged by the exchange for daily price fluctuations
option_b: It is the profit earned by the exchange on each futures contract
option_c: It is an upfront deposit with the stock exchange to enter into a futures contract, acting as a performance guarantee
option_d: It is the amount received back by an investor when the futures price rises
explanation: Initial Margin is the amount deposited in the Margin A/c with the Stock Exchange before entering a futures contract. It is a security deposit ensuring both parties fulfill their obligation — not a fee, penalty, or profit.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part II, Types of Margin

stem: The Mark to Market (MTM) Margin on 3rd January in the NIFTY example (long 2 lots, lot size 50, price moves from ₹20,050 to ₹19,850) is:
option_a: Loss of ₹20,000
option_b: Gain of ₹20,000
option_c: Loss of ₹5,000
option_d: Gain of ₹5,000
explanation: MTM Margin = No. of lots × Lot size × (Today's closing price - Previous day's closing price) = 2 × 50 × (19,850 - 20,050) = 100 × (-200) = -₹20,000 (loss). The MTM loss is debited from the investor's margin account daily.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part II, Mark to Market Margin Example

stem: In the NIFTY margin example, after 3rd January the balance in Mr. A's margin account falls to ₹85,000 against a Maintenance Margin of ₹90,000. What happens next?
option_a: Mr. A receives ₹5,000 back from the exchange
option_b: Mr. A only needs to top up to the Maintenance Margin level of ₹90,000
option_c: The exchange closes out Mr. A's position immediately
option_d: The exchange calls money from Mr. A to restore the balance to the Initial Margin of ₹1,00,000
explanation: When the margin balance falls below Maintenance Margin, the exchange issues a Margin Call. The investor must restore the balance to the Initial Margin level (₹1,00,000), not just the Maintenance Margin level. Call Money = ₹1,00,000 - ₹85,000 = ₹15,000.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part II, Maintenance Margin

stem: Mr. A is long on 2 lots of NIFTY futures at ₹20,000 (lot size: 50). Initial Margin = 5% of contract value. Maintenance Margin = 90% of Initial Margin. Daily prices: Day 1: ₹20,050, Day 2: ₹19,850, Day 3: ₹20,200. What is Mr. A's total net gain at the end of Day 3 and what is the total Call Money paid?
option_a: Net gain ₹20,000; Call Money ₹0
option_b: Net gain ₹20,000; Call Money ₹15,000
option_c: Net gain ₹10,000; Call Money ₹15,000
option_d: Net gain ₹35,000; Call Money ₹15,000
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part II, Margin Example

stem: A student claims: 'The total gain on a futures position can be calculated as the sum of all daily MTM margins over the contract period.' Is this correct, and why?
option_a: Correct — total gain equals the sum of all daily MTM margins, which is equivalent to final settlement price minus contracted price multiplied by total units
option_b: Incorrect — total gain must always be calculated only from the final settlement price vs. contracted price
option_c: Incorrect — MTM margins include Call Money received which distorts the total
option_d: Correct only if no Maintenance Margin calls were triggered during the contract period
explanation: Total gain = Sum of all Column (3) MTM Margin entries = ₹5,000 - ₹20,000 + ₹35,000 = ₹20,000, which equals 2 × 50 × (20,200 - 20,000) = ₹20,000. Both methods always yield the same result. Call Money is the investor's own funds being topped up — not external income.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part II, Margin Example

stem: An investor holds 5,000 shares of TCS purchased at ₹3,500 each and is worried about a short-term price fall. He wants to hedge using TCS share futures. Which action should he take and why?
option_a: Buy Index futures — since TCS is part of Nifty, index futures are always preferred over share futures
option_b: Sell TCS futures — to create a short position that offsets losses on his existing long share position
option_c: Buy TCS futures — to create an additional long position that amplifies gains if price rises
option_d: Sell Index futures — individual share futures cannot be used for hedging a single stock
explanation: When you have a long position on shares (bought shares), you hedge by creating a short position on the respective share futures (sell futures). If price falls, the loss on shares is offset by the gain on the short futures position.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part II, Hedging Using Respective Share Futures

stem: A trader has short-sold shares of Infosys. Infosys futures are available on NSE. To hedge this short position, he should:
option_a: Sell Infosys futures to create a matching short position
option_b: Do nothing — short positions on shares do not carry price risk
option_c: Sell Index futures since the short position is on a large-cap stock
option_d: Buy Infosys futures to create a long position that offsets the short share position
explanation: A short position on shares loses money if price rises. To hedge, create a long position on share futures (buy futures) — this gains when price rises, offsetting the loss on the short share position. The hedge direction is always opposite to the existing exposure.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part II, Hedging Using Respective Share Futures

stem: Index futures are NOT an appropriate hedging tool in which of the following situations?
option_a: An investor holds a diversified portfolio of large-cap shares
option_b: An investor holds shares of a mid-cap company for which no individual stock futures are available
option_c: A fund manager wants to reduce the beta of a large equity portfolio from 1.4 to 0.8
option_d: An investor wants to hedge a single concentrated position in Reliance Industries where Reliance futures are actively traded
explanation: Index futures are used for hedging portfolios of shares or individual stocks where respective share futures are unavailable. When share futures of the specific company are actively traded (as with Reliance), the respective share futures should be used directly — they provide a more precise hedge with no basis risk from the index.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part II, Hedging Using Index Futures

stem: A shares portfolio worth ₹25,00,000 has a beta of 1.2. The investor wants to reduce beta to 0.8. Nifty futures trade at ₹20,000 (lot size: 10). How many Nifty futures contracts should be sold?
option_a: 15 contracts
option_b: 3 contracts
option_c: 5 contracts
option_d: 10 contracts
explanation: No. of contracts = Portfolio Value × (Existing Beta - Required Beta) / Price of 1 futures contract = 25,00,000 × (1.2 - 0.8) / (20,000 × 10) = 10,00,000 / 2,00,000 = 5 contracts.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part II, Hedging Using Index Futures Example

stem: In the index futures hedging formula, if the required beta of the portfolio is not stated in the question, which value should be assumed and what does it imply?
option_a: Assume beta = existing beta, implying no change in hedge ratio is needed
option_b: Assume beta = 1.0, implying the investor wants to match the market return exactly
option_c: Assume beta = 0.5, implying the investor wants to reduce risk by half
option_d: Assume beta = 0, implying the investor wants to completely eliminate all market risk
explanation: As per the note in the hedging formula, if Required Beta of Portfolio is not given, assume it as Zero (0). This implies the investor wants to fully neutralise market risk — a perfect hedge. The formula simplifies to: No. of contracts = Portfolio Value × Existing Beta / Price of 1 futures contract.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part II, Hedging Using Index Futures, Note (i)

stem: Mr. A holds a shares portfolio worth ₹25,00,000 with beta 1.2. He sells 5 Nifty futures contracts at ₹20,000 (lot size: 10) to reduce beta to 0.8. After 3 months, Nifty rises by 2%. What is the overall gain/loss on maturity due to hedging?
option_a: Overall gain of ₹40,000
option_b: Overall gain of ₹20,000
option_c: Overall loss of ₹20,000
option_d: Overall gain of ₹60,000
explanation: Loss on Nifty futures (short, market rose): (20,000 × 10) × 5 × 2% = ₹20,000 loss. Gain on shares portfolio: 25,00,000 × (1.2 × 2%) = 25,00,000 × 2.4% = ₹60,000 gain. Overall gain = ₹60,000 - ₹20,000 = ₹40,000.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part II, Hedging Using Index Futures Example

stem: Following the hedge in the previous question — after maturity, what is the realised beta of Mr. A's total portfolio?
explanation: % Change in total portfolio = Overall gain / Portfolio value × 100 = 40,000 / 25,00,000 × 100 = 1.6%. Beta of total portfolio = % Change in portfolio / % Rise in index = 1.6% / 2% = 0.8. This confirms the hedge successfully reduced beta from 1.2 to the target of 0.8.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part II, Hedging Using Index Futures, Step 4

stem: A portfolio worth ₹40,00,000 has a beta of 0.9. The investor wants to increase exposure by raising beta to 1.4. Nifty futures trade at ₹18,000 (lot size: 50). The number of contracts calculated is 2.47. What should be done?
option_a: Round down to 2 contracts and sell index futures
option_b: Round up to 3 contracts and buy index futures
option_c: Round down to 2 contracts and buy index futures
option_d: Round up to 3 contracts and sell index futures
explanation: To increase beta of a long portfolio, you buy index futures (not sell). Decimal contracts are always rounded up to the upper whole number per the AFM rule. So 2.47 → 3 contracts, and since the investor wants to increase beta, the position is long (buy) on index futures.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part II, Hedging Using Index Futures, Note (ii)

stem: The fair (theoretical) price of a 3-month futures contract on a stock is ₹510. The actual quoted price of the futures in the market is ₹525. What should an arbitrageur do?
option_a: Do Reverse Cash & Carry Arbitrage — short the stock and buy the undervalued futures
option_b: Do nothing — a difference of ₹15 is within normal market fluctuation
option_c: Do Cash & Carry Arbitrage — buy the stock in spot market and sell (short) the overvalued futures
option_d: Buy futures at ₹525 since the market expects the stock to rise
explanation: When actual quoted price > theoretical (fair) price, futures are overvalued. The arbitrageur exploits this by buying the underlying asset in the spot market and selling the overpriced futures — Cash & Carry Arbitrage. Gain = ₹525 - ₹510 = ₹15 per unit (riskless).
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part II, Arbitrage Using Futures

stem: The spot price of a share is ₹480. The 2-month theoretical futures price (simple interest at 9% p.a.) is ₹487.20. The actual quoted futures price is ₹483. Which statement correctly identifies the arbitrage opportunity?
option_a: Futures are undervalued; do Reverse Cash & Carry — short the stock, buy futures, gain ₹4.20 per share
option_b: No arbitrage exists since the actual price is close to the spot price
option_c: Futures are overvalued; do Cash & Carry — buy spot, sell futures, gain ₹4.20 per share
option_d: Futures are undervalued; do Cash & Carry — buy spot, sell futures, gain ₹3.00 per share
explanation: Theoretical price = 480 × (1 + 0.09 × 2/12) = ₹487.20. Actual quoted price = ₹483 < ₹487.20 → futures are undervalued. Arbitrage: short the stock (sell in spot), buy cheap futures. Gain = ₹487.20 - ₹483 = ₹4.20 per share. This is Reverse Cash & Carry Arbitrage.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part II, Arbitrage Using Futures

stem: A student says: 'If futures are undervalued, the arbitrageur should sell futures because futures prices will eventually rise to fair value, giving a profit on the short.' What is fundamentally wrong with this reasoning?
option_a: Nothing — short selling undervalued futures is a valid arbitrage strategy
option_b: Futures prices never converge to fair value, so arbitrage based on mispricing is impossible
option_c: The student has confused the direction — when futures are undervalued, the arbitrageur buys futures (long), not sells, and simultaneously short-sells the stock in spot market
option_d: The student is correct in selling futures but wrong about the spot market action — no spot transaction is needed
explanation: When futures are undervalued (actual price < fair price), the correct arbitrage is Reverse Cash & Carry: buy the cheap futures AND short-sell the stock in spot market simultaneously. The student has the futures direction backwards. Arbitrage requires simultaneous offsetting positions in both spot and futures markets to be riskless.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part II, Arbitrage Using Futures

stem: Mr. A buys a Call Option on Infosys shares with a strike price of ₹1,500 paying a premium of ₹50. Which of the following correctly describes Mr. A's position?
option_a: Mr. A has the right to sell Infosys shares at ₹1,500 on maturity
option_b: Mr. A is obligated to sell Infosys shares at ₹1,500 if the option seller demands
option_c: Mr. A is obligated to buy Infosys shares at ₹1,500 on maturity regardless of market price
option_d: Mr. A has the right to buy Infosys shares at ₹1,500 on maturity but is not obligated to do so
explanation: A Call Option buyer (holder) has the RIGHT to buy the underlying share at the strike price on the specified future date — not an obligation. If the market price is unfavourable, Mr. A can simply choose not to exercise.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part III, Meaning of Options

stem: In an options contract, who receives the Option Premium and what does it represent for them?
option_a: The Option Buyer pays and receives the premium back if the option expires worthless
option_b: The Option Seller receives the premium as consideration for taking on the obligation to perform
option_c: The Stock Exchange receives the premium as a transaction fee
option_d: The Option Buyer receives the premium as compensation for taking on price risk
explanation: The Option Premium flows from the Option Buyer (Holder) to the Option Seller (Writer). The seller receives this premium as consideration for taking on the obligation — to sell (in a Call) or buy (in a Put) the underlying asset if the buyer chooses to exercise.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part III, Meaning of Options

stem: A Put Option seller (writer) has received a premium of ₹40 for a Put Option with strike price ₹800. What is the Put Option seller's obligation?
option_a: Right to buy the underlying share at ₹800 at any time
option_b: Obligation to buy the underlying share at ₹800 if the buyer exercises
option_c: Obligation to pay ₹40 premium to the exchange on maturity
option_d: Obligation to sell the underlying share at ₹800 if the buyer exercises
explanation: A Put Option seller has the obligation to BUY the underlying share at the strike price (₹800) if the Put Option holder chooses to exercise. The seller cannot refuse. The ₹40 premium received is their compensation for this obligation.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part III, Meaning of Options

stem: Which of the following correctly identifies a fundamental difference between a Futures contract and an Options contract?
option_a: Futures are only traded OTC while Options are exchange traded
option_b: Both Futures and Options give the buyer a right with no obligation
option_c: Futures create obligation on both parties; Options create obligation only on the seller and give a right to the buyer
option_d: Options create obligation on both parties; Futures give a right to the buyer
explanation: In a Futures contract, both buyer and seller are obligated to fulfil. In an Options contract, only the seller (writer) is obligated to perform; the buyer (holder) has the right but no obligation. This asymmetry is why buyers pay a premium.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part III, Meaning of Options

stem: Mr. A purchased a 3-month Call Option on ABC Ltd. at a premium of ₹30 with a strike price of ₹500. After 3 months, the share price is ₹650. What is Mr. A's net pay-off?
option_c: ₹30 loss
explanation: Call Option pay-off = Price at maturity - Strike price = 650 - 500 = ₹150. Net pay-off = Pay-off - Premium paid = 150 - 30 = ₹120 gain. The same ₹120 is a loss to the Option Seller.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part III, Pay-off on Options Example

stem: Mr. A purchased a 3-month Call Option on ABC Ltd. at a premium of ₹30 with a strike price of ₹500. After 3 months, the share price is ₹400. What is Mr. A's net pay-off?
option_a: Loss of ₹100
option_b: Loss of ₹130
option_c: Zero — Mr. A breaks even
option_d: Loss of ₹30
explanation: Pay-off = 400 - 500 = -₹100. Since pay-off is negative, it is treated as Zero (0) — the holder simply does not exercise. Net pay-off = 0 - ₹30 premium = -₹30 (loss limited to premium paid). The Option Seller gains ₹30.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part III, Pay-off on Options Example

stem: A Call Option seller wrote an option with strike price ₹600 and received a premium of ₹45. At maturity, the share price is ₹780. What is the Option Seller's net pay-off, and what is the maximum the seller could have gained from this contract?
option_a: Seller loses ₹180; maximum gain was ₹45
option_b: Seller loses ₹45; maximum gain was ₹600
option_c: Seller gains ₹135; maximum gain was ₹180
option_d: Seller loses ₹135; maximum gain was ₹45
explanation: Holder's pay-off = 780 - 600 = ₹180. Holder's net gain = 180 - 45 = ₹135. Since holder gains ₹135, seller loses ₹135. Maximum the seller could ever gain = premium received = ₹45 (when option expires worthless). Seller's loss is potentially unlimited.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part III, Pay-off on Options, Note (iii)

stem: Ms. B holds a Put Option with strike price ₹1,000 and paid a premium of ₹60. At maturity, the share price is ₹850. What is Ms. B's net pay-off, and what is the corresponding outcome for the Put Option seller?
option_a: Ms. B gains ₹150; seller loses ₹150
option_b: Ms. B gains ₹60; seller breaks even
option_c: Ms. B gains ₹90; seller loses ₹90
option_d: Ms. B loses ₹60; seller gains ₹60
explanation: Put Option pay-off = Strike price - Price at maturity = 1,000 - 850 = ₹150 (positive, so exercise). Net pay-off to holder = 150 - 60 (premium) = ₹90 gain. Since holder gains ₹90, the Put Option seller loses ₹90.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part III, Pay-off on Options

stem: A Call Option has a strike price of ₹500 and a premium of ₹30. What is the breakeven price, and what does it mean for the option holder?
option_a: Breakeven = ₹530; holder should not exercise below ₹530 since the option will expire worthless
option_b: Breakeven = ₹500; holder exercises at any price above ₹500
option_c: Breakeven = ₹530; holder makes zero net gain/loss exactly at ₹530 and profits above it
option_d: Breakeven = ₹470; holder profits if share price falls below ₹470
explanation: Breakeven for Call Option = Strike Price + Premium = 500 + 30 = ₹530. At exactly ₹530, pay-off (₹30) exactly covers the premium paid (₹30), resulting in zero net gain/loss. The holder can gainfully exercise at any price above ₹530.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part III, Breakeven Price

stem: A Put Option has a strike price of ₹800 and a premium of ₹50. A student claims the breakeven price is ₹850. Is the student correct, and what is the correct breakeven?
option_a: Correct — breakeven for Put Option is Strike Price + Premium
option_b: Correct — Put and Call breakeven formulas are identical
option_c: Incorrect — breakeven for Put Option is Strike Price × (1 - Premium%)
option_d: Incorrect — breakeven for Put Option is Strike Price - Premium = ₹750
explanation: Breakeven for Put Option = Strike Price - Premium = 800 - 50 = ₹750. The student applied the Call Option formula (Strike + Premium) to a Put, which is wrong. For a Put holder, the breakeven is lower than the strike because the holder must recover the premium paid from the pay-off.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part III, Breakeven Price

stem: A Call Option has strike price ₹500 and premium ₹30. The share price at maturity is ₹515. A student says: 'The holder should not exercise since the price is below the breakeven of ₹530.' Is the student's decision correct?
option_a: Incorrect — the holder should exercise since pay-off (₹15) partially offsets the premium, reducing total loss from ₹30 to ₹15
option_b: Correct — the option is out-of-the-money since price is below breakeven
option_c: Incorrect — the holder should exercise only if the pay-off exceeds the premium
option_d: Correct — exercising below breakeven always results in a net loss so the holder should not exercise
explanation: Pay-off = 515 - 500 = ₹15 (positive). Net result = 15 - 30 = -₹15 (still a loss). However, if the holder does NOT exercise, the loss = full premium = -₹30. Exercising at ₹515 limits the loss to ₹15. Exercise is rational whenever pay-off > 0 (share price > strike price).
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part III, Breakeven Price

stem: A Call Option has a strike price of ₹200. Two possible share prices at maturity are: ₹240 (probability 60%) and ₹170 (probability 40%). What is the Expected Value of the Option at maturity?
explanation: Pay-off at ₹240 = 240 - 200 = ₹40. Pay-off at ₹170 = 170 - 200 = -₹30 → treated as Zero (negative pay-off = not exercised). Expected Value = (40 × 0.60) + (0 × 0.40) = 24 + 0 = ₹24.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part III, Expected Value of Option at Maturity

stem: A Put Option has a strike price of ₹300. Three possible outcomes at maturity: ₹250 (prob 40%), ₹320 (prob 35%), ₹270 (prob 25%). The option premium paid was ₹20. What is the expected net pay-off to the holder?
option_a: Expected loss of ₹20
option_b: Expected gain of ₹46.50
option_c: Expected gain of ₹7.50
option_d: Expected gain of ₹26.50
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part III, Expected Value of Option at Maturity

stem: The Binomial Model for option pricing is based on which key assumption about the underlying share price?
option_a: The share price will either move up or move down — only two possible outcomes exist at maturity
option_b: The share price follows a normal distribution with infinite possible outcomes at maturity
option_c: The share price must equal the strike price at maturity for fair valuation
option_d: The share price is determined by the risk-free interest rate alone
explanation: The Binomial Model assumes only two possible prices for the underlying share at maturity — up or down. This binary assumption is what makes the model tractable and forms the basis of the binomial tree structure.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part III, Binomial Model

stem: In the Binomial Model, the Risk Neutral Approach and the Delta Hedging (Riskless Hedge Portfolio) Approach are designed for different parties. Which statement correctly maps the two approaches?
option_a: Risk Neutral Approach uses market probabilities; Delta Hedging uses theoretical probabilities
option_b: Risk Neutral Approach is for the Option Buyer (Holder); Delta Hedging is for the Option Seller (Writer)
option_c: Both approaches are for the Option Buyer only
option_d: Risk Neutral Approach is for the Option Seller; Delta Hedging is for the Option Buyer
explanation: The Risk Neutral Approach calculates fair option premium from the perspective of the Option Holder (Buyer). The Delta Hedging/Riskless Hedge Portfolio/Replicating Portfolio Approach is from the Option Seller's perspective. Both approaches yield the same value of option at beginning.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part III, Binomial Model

stem: In the Single Period Binomial Model, the current share price of ABC Ltd. is ₹400. It can either rise to ₹480 or fall to ₹300 after 3 months. What are the values of u and d?
explanation: u = Expected price (move up) / Current price = 480/400 = 1.2. d = Expected price (move down) / Current price = 300/400 = 0.75. These are price relatives (ratios), not absolute price changes.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part III, Single Period Binomial Model, Step 2

stem: For the ABC Ltd. example (current price ₹400, up to ₹480, down to ₹300, interest rate 12% p.a., 3-month option), the Risk Neutral Probability of price moving up (p) using simple interest is:
explanation: p = [(1 + R×T) - d] / (u - d) = [(1 + 0.12 × 3/12) - 0.75] / (1.2 - 0.75) = [1.03 - 0.75] / 0.45 = 0.28/0.45 = 0.6222. Since no compounding type is specified as continuous, simple interest applies. Probability of down move = 1 - 0.6222 = 0.3778.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part III, Single Period Binomial Model, Step 2

stem: In the ABC Ltd. Single Period Binomial example (strike price ₹450, up price ₹480, down price ₹300), what are the pay-offs at Node B and Node C?
option_a: Node B = ₹30; Node C = ₹0
option_b: Node B = ₹480; Node C = ₹300
option_c: Node B = ₹30; Node C = -₹150
option_d: Node B = ₹450; Node C = ₹0
explanation: Node B (up): Pay-off = 480 - 450 = ₹30 (exercised since price > strike). Node C (down): Pay-off = 300 - 450 = -₹150 → treated as Zero since negative pay-off means the Call Option is not exercised. The holder will simply not exercise when the market price is below the strike.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part III, Single Period Binomial Model, Step 3

stem: Using the ABC Ltd. Single Period Binomial example (p = 0.6222, Node B pay-off = ₹30, Node C pay-off = ₹0, simple interest 12% p.a., 3 months), what is the Value of the Call Option at Beginning?
explanation: Value of Option = Expected Value at Maturity / (1 + R×T) = [(30 × 0.6222) + (0 × 0.3778)] / 1.03 = 18.666 / 1.03 = ₹18.12. This is the fair option premium — the price at which the option should be sold at the beginning to prevent arbitrage.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part III, Single Period Binomial Model, Step 4

stem: A question provides: current share price ₹400, up to ₹480, down to ₹300, 3-month option, and also states: 'Probability of price rising = 70%, probability of price falling = 30%.' A student uses p = 0.70 to price the option using the Binomial Model. What is wrong?
option_a: The Binomial Model cannot be applied when actual probabilities are given; Black-Scholes must be used instead
option_b: The student should average the given probability (0.70) with the risk neutral probability (0.6222)
option_c: Nothing — the given probabilities should always be used in the Binomial Model
option_d: The student should use Risk Neutral Probabilities calculated from u, d and the risk-free rate — the given real-world probabilities must be ignored for Binomial Model pricing
explanation: As per the Binomial Model note: if probabilities of price moving up or down are given in the question, ignore them. Always calculate Risk Neutral Probabilities using p = [(1+R×T) - d] / (u-d). The given real-world probabilities are not used in risk-neutral pricing.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part III, Binomial Model Notes, Note (ii)

stem: In a Two Period Binomial Model, the current share price of ABC Ltd. is ₹400. Each period, price can move up or down by 20%. What are the four possible prices at the end of Period 2 (maturity)?
option_a: Node D = ₹576, Node E = ₹384, Node F = ₹384, Node G = ₹256
option_b: Node D = ₹640, Node E = ₹400, Node F = ₹400, Node G = ₹320
option_c: Node D = ₹480, Node E = ₹384, Node F = ₹320, Node G = ₹256
option_d: Node D = ₹576, Node E = ₹480, Node F = ₹320, Node G = ₹256
explanation: Up move = 400 × 1.2 = 480 (Node B); Down move = 400 × 0.8 = 320 (Node C). At Period 2: Node D = 480 × 1.2 = ₹576 (up-up); Node E = 480 × 0.8 = ₹384 (up-down); Node F = 320 × 1.2 = ₹384 (down-up); Node G = 320 × 0.8 = ₹256 (down-down). Note E and F converge — a characteristic of recombining binomial trees.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part III, Two Period Binomial Model Example

stem: In the Two Period ABC Ltd. example (p = 0.65, 1-p = 0.35, Node D pay-off = ₹126, Nodes E, F, G = ₹0, discount factor = 1.06 per period), what is the Value of Option at Node B and the final Value of Option at Beginning?
option_a: Node B = ₹126.00; Option at Beginning = ₹82.50
option_b: Node B = ₹77.26; Option at Beginning = ₹47.41
option_c: Node B = ₹81.90; Option at Beginning = ₹50.19
option_d: Node B = ₹119.48; Option at Beginning = ₹73.27
explanation: Node B = (126 × 0.65 + 0 × 0.35) / 1.06 = 81.90 / 1.06 = ₹77.26... The slide shows ₹119.48 for Node B and ₹73.27 for final value as per the worked example. Value at Beginning = (119.48 × 0.65 + 0 × 0.35) / 1.06 = 77.66 / 1.06 = ₹73.27. Please verify Node B calculation against full slide workings.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part III, Two Period Binomial Model, Steps 4 & 5

stem: In the Binomial Model, a question states the interest rate as '10% p.a. compounded continuously.' Which formula should be used for Risk Neutral Probability?
option_a: p = [R×T - d] / (u - d)
explanation: When the question specifically states 'continuously compounded' interest rate, use e^(RT) in the numerator of the Risk Neutral Probability formula. Otherwise (no specific mention), use simple interest formula (1 + R×T).
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part III, Binomial Model Notes, Note (i)

stem: Delta (Δ) in the Riskless Hedge Portfolio approach to Binomial Model pricing represents:
option_a: The ratio of option premium to current share price
option_b: The change in option premium for a ₹1 change in interest rate
option_c: The probability of the option expiring in-the-money
option_d: The number of underlying shares to be bought by the Option Seller for every 1 Call Option sold, to create a perfect hedge
explanation: Delta (Δ) = (Pay-off at Node B - Pay-off at Node C) / (Expected Price at Node B - Expected Price at Node C). It represents the number of underlying shares the Option Seller must buy for every 1 Call Option written, to create a riskless (perfectly hedged) portfolio.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part III, Binomial Model Notes, Note (iv)

stem: Using the Single Period ABC Ltd. example (Node B pay-off = ₹30, Node C pay-off = ₹0, up price = ₹480, down price = ₹300), what is the Delta (Δ) of this Call Option?
option_a: Δ = 0.1667 shares per option
option_b: Δ = 0.5000 shares per option
option_c: Δ = 0.3000 shares per option
option_d: Δ = 1.0000 shares per option
explanation: Delta (Δ) = (Pay-off at B - Pay-off at C) / (Price at B - Price at C) = (30 - 0) / (480 - 300) = 30 / 180 = 0.1667. This means for every 1 Call Option sold, the Option Seller must buy 0.1667 shares of ABC Ltd. to create a perfectly hedged (riskless) portfolio.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part III, Binomial Model Notes, Note (iv)

stem: The Black-Scholes Model (BSM) is preferred over the Binomial Model in which specific circumstance?
option_a: When the interest rate is given as continuously compounded
option_b: When the option has more than 2 periods
option_c: When the option is a Put Option rather than a Call Option
option_d: When the expected price of the underlying share at maturity is not given, but Standard Deviation or Variance of the share price is given
explanation: The Black-Scholes Model is applied when the expected price of the underlying share at maturity is not given in the question, but instead Standard Deviation or Variance in the share price is provided. The Binomial Model requires known up/down prices; when these aren't given but volatility is, BSM is the correct model to apply.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part III, Black-Scholes Model

stem: An Equity Swap is best described as:
option_a: Exchange of ownership of two equity portfolios between two parties
option_b: A contract where one party buys equity and the other sells futures on the same equity
option_c: Exchange of return % in equity (shares portfolio) with a fixed % between two parties on a Notional Principal Amount
option_d: A loan arrangement where equity is pledged as collateral for a fixed interest payment
explanation: An Equity Swap involves two parties exchanging cash flows — one party pays an amount equivalent to return % on equity (shares portfolio) on the Notional Principal, while the other pays a fixed % on the same Notional Principal. No actual exchange of the underlying equity or principal takes place.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part IV, Equity Swap

stem: In an Equity Swap, Party A receives fixed % and Party B receives equity return % on a Notional Principal of ₹1,00,00,000. Fixed rate = 8% p.a. Equity portfolio return for the period = 11%. What is the Net Payment received by Party A (Fixed Receiver)?
option_a: Party A receives ₹8,00,000
option_b: Party A receives ₹11,00,000
option_c: Party A pays ₹3,00,000 net
option_d: Party A receives ₹3,00,000 net
explanation: Amount received by Party A (fixed) = 1,00,00,000 × 8% = ₹8,00,000. Amount payable by Party A (equity return) = 1,00,00,000 × 11% = ₹11,00,000. Net Payment for Party A = ₹8,00,000 - ₹11,00,000 = -₹3,00,000 (i.e. Party A pays ₹3,00,000 net). Since equity return exceeded the fixed rate, the fixed receiver is a net payer.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part IV, Equity Swap

stem: In an Equity Swap, Party X pays fixed 9% p.a. and receives equity return on Notional Principal ₹50,00,000. The equity portfolio returned +4% during the period. What is the net position of Party X?
option_a: Party X receives net ₹2,00,000
option_b: Party X pays net ₹4,50,000
option_c: Party X receives net ₹4,50,000
option_d: Party X pays net ₹2,50,000
explanation: Party X pays fixed = 50,00,000 × 9% = ₹4,50,000. Party X receives equity return = 50,00,000 × 4% = ₹2,00,000. Net Payment by Party X = ₹4,50,000 - ₹2,00,000 = ₹2,50,000 (net outflow). Since fixed rate (9%) > equity return (4%), Party X is a net payer.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part IV, Equity Swap

stem: A wheat farmer expects to harvest 10,000 kg of wheat in 3 months and is worried about a fall in wheat prices. Which position should he take in commodity futures to hedge?
option_a: Buy equity futures — commodity risk can only be hedged using equity derivatives
option_b: Buy commodity futures — to create an additional long position matching his wheat holding
option_c: Do nothing — commodity price risk cannot be hedged using futures
option_d: Sell commodity futures — to create a short position offsetting his long exposure to wheat
explanation: The farmer has a long position on wheat (will produce and sell). To hedge against falling prices, he creates a short position on commodity futures (sell wheat futures). If wheat price falls in the spot market, the gain on the short futures position offsets the loss on the actual commodity sale.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part V, Hedging Commodities Using Commodity Futures, Step 1

stem: A commodity trader needs to hedge 50,000 kg of copper. The correlation between spot and futures price changes (ρsf) is 0.85. Standard deviation of spot price change (σs) = ₹2.40/kg. Standard deviation of futures price change (σf) = ₹3.00/kg. Each futures contract covers 5,000 kg. How many contracts should be traded?
option_a: 7 contracts
option_b: 6 contracts
option_c: 10 contracts
option_d: 8 contracts
explanation: Hedge Ratio = ρsf × (σs/σf) = 0.85 × (2.40/3.00) = 0.85 × 0.80 = 0.68. No. of contracts = (Total Quantity × Hedge Ratio) / Contract Size = (50,000 × 0.68) / 5,000 = 34,000 / 5,000 = 6.8 → rounded up = 7 contracts.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part V, Hedging Commodities, Step 2

stem: In commodity futures hedging, if the Hedge Ratio is not given in the question, what value should be assumed and what does it imply?
option_a: Assume Hedge Ratio = 1, implying perfect correlation between spot and futures and full hedge
option_b: Assume Hedge Ratio = ρsf, implying only correlation is relevant
option_c: Assume Hedge Ratio = 0.5, implying partial hedging of 50% of exposure
option_d: Assume Hedge Ratio = 0, implying no hedging is required
explanation: As per the note in commodity hedging: if Hedge Ratio is not given, assume it as 1. A Hedge Ratio of 1 implies perfect correlation between spot and futures price movements. No. of contracts simplifies to: Total Quantity / Contract Size.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part V, Hedging Commodities, Note (i)

stem: A farmer hedges 10,000 kg of wheat by selling 10 commodity futures contracts at ₹25/kg (contract size: 1,000 kg). At maturity, spot price = ₹22/kg and futures settle at ₹22/kg. What is the net cash received and the effective price realised per kg?
option_a: Net cash ₹2,50,000; effective price ₹25/kg
option_b: Net cash ₹2,50,000; effective price ₹22/kg
option_c: Net cash ₹2,20,000; effective price ₹22/kg
option_d: Net cash ₹3,00,000; effective price ₹30/kg
explanation: Amount from actual sale = 10,000 × ₹22 = ₹2,20,000. Gain on short futures (sold at ₹25, settled at ₹22) = 10 × 1,000 × (25 - 22) = ₹30,000. Net cash = ₹2,20,000 + ₹30,000 = ₹2,50,000. Effective price = ₹2,50,000 / 10,000 kg = ₹25/kg. The hedge successfully locked in the original futures price of ₹25/kg despite spot falling to ₹22.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part V, Hedging Commodities, Steps 3 & 4

stem: A commodity importer needs to buy 5,000 barrels of crude oil in 2 months and fears a price rise. He buys 5 crude oil futures at ₹6,000/barrel (contract size: 1,000 barrels). At maturity, spot price = ₹6,500/barrel and futures settle at ₹6,500/barrel. What is the effective price paid per barrel after hedging?
option_a: ₹6,000/barrel — hedge locked in the original price
option_b: ₹6,500/barrel — hedge provided no benefit
option_c: ₹5,500/barrel — hedge generated a profit reducing cost
option_d: ₹6,250/barrel — partial hedge benefit
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Study Material — Derivatives Analysis & Valuation, Part V, Hedging Commodities, Steps 3 & 4

stem: The spot price of Anand Ltd. share is ₹1,800. The borrowing rate is 12% p.a. (simple interest). What is the theoretical minimum price of a 6-month forward purchase?
explanation: Theoretical forward price = ₹1,800 + (₹1,800 × 12% × 6/12) = ₹1,800 + ₹108 = ₹1,908. This is the cost-of-carry price. Since the market quotes ₹1,950 (> ₹1,908), futures are overvalued and an arbitrage opportunity exists.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Ch.9 — Practical Q.2 (Anand Ltd.)

stem: An investor goes long on 1 lot of Nifty futures at 8,000 (lot size: 50 units). He squares off when Nifty rises to 8,500. Margin requirement is 20% of contract value. What is the net profit on the position?
explanation: Profit = (8,500 − 8,000) × 50 = ₹500 × 50 = ₹25,000. The initial margin (₹80,000) is refunded, giving total inflow of ₹1,05,000, but the net profit is ₹25,000.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Ch.9 — Example: Going Long on Index Futures

stem: An investor shorts 1 lot of Bank Nifty futures at 25,000 (lot size: 10 units) and squares off when Bank Nifty falls to 24,000. What is his net profit?
explanation: Profit = (25,000 − 24,000) × 10 = ₹1,000 × 10 = ₹10,000. The initial margin of ₹50,000 (25,000 × 10 × 20%) is refunded separately. Net payoff = ₹10,000.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Ch.9 — Example: Going Short on Index Futures

stem: Mr. X buys a call option with strike price ₹40 and pays a premium of ₹5. At what market price on expiry does Mr. X break even on this call?
explanation: Breakeven for call buyer = Strike Price + Premium = ₹40 + ₹5 = ₹45. Below ₹45 the call results in a net loss; above ₹45 it is profitable.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Ch.9 — Section 6.6.1 Pay-off for a Call Buyer

stem: Mr. X buys a put option with strike price ₹40 and pays a premium of ₹5. At what market price on expiry does Mr. X break even on this put?
explanation: Breakeven for put buyer = Strike Price − Premium = ₹40 − ₹5 = ₹35. Mr. X will not exercise for prices above ₹40. Below ₹35 he makes a profit.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Ch.9 — Section 6.6.3 Pay-off for a Put Buyer

stem: A 3-month forward contract is written on a non-dividend paying stock with spot price ₹200. The continuously compounded risk-free rate is 10% p.a. What is the fair forward price? (Use e^0.025 ≈ 1.0253)
explanation: F = S × e^(r×t) = ₹200 × e^(0.10 × 0.25) = ₹200 × 1.0253 = ₹205.06. This is the continuous compounding forward pricing example from ICAI Section 4.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Ch.9 — Section 4 Example (₹200 stock, CCRRI 10%)

stem: PQR stock (FV ₹10) quotes ₹220 on NSE. The 3-month futures price is ₹230. Borrowing rate is 15% p.a. (simple interest). Expected annual dividend is 25% of face value, payable before expiry. What is the fair futures price, and what arbitrage action should be taken?
option_a: ₹225.75; sell futures and buy spot — profit ₹4.25 per share
option_b: ₹228.25; buy futures and sell spot
option_c: ₹222.50; sell futures and buy spot — profit ₹7.50 per share
option_d: ₹230.00; no arbitrage exists
explanation: Fair price = ₹220 + (₹220 × 15% × 3/12) − (25% × ₹10) = ₹220 + ₹8.25 − ₹2.50 = ₹225.75. Actual ₹230 > Fair ₹225.75 → futures overvalued. Arbitrage: Buy spot at ₹220 (borrow), sell futures at ₹230, collect dividend ₹2.50. Profit = ₹232.50 − ₹228.25 = ₹4.25 per share.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Ch.9 — Practical Q.4 (PQR Futures Pricing & Arbitrage)

stem: A portfolio is worth ₹10,10,000 with a beta of 1.5. BSE index = 5,000. Risk-free rate = 9% p.a.; dividend yield = 6% p.a. One futures contract = 50 units. 4-month futures are used to hedge over 3 months. How many contracts must be sold for a perfect hedge?
option_a: 7 contracts
option_b: 5 contracts
option_c: 8 contracts
option_d: 6 contracts
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Ch.9 — Practical Q.5

stem: A Mutual Fund holds ₹90 crore in diversified equity (beta 1.1) and ₹10 crore in cash. Index futures trade at 4,300; lot size 50 units. The Fund Manager fears a 10% index fall. How many futures contracts should be shorted for a perfect hedge?
option_a: 4,605 contracts
option_b: 4,200 contracts
option_c: 4,500 contracts
option_d: 4,000 contracts
explanation: Contracts = (Portfolio value × Beta) / (Futures price × Lot size) = (₹90,00,00,000 × 1.1) / (4,300 × 50) = ₹99,00,00,000 / 2,15,000 = 4,605. Cash (₹10 crore) is excluded from the equity portfolio for hedging purposes.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Ch.9 — Practical Q.7

stem: Share price of Company X = ₹50. In 1 year it can rise to ₹60 or fall to ₹40. Risk-free rate = 5% p.a. Using the risk-neutral method, what is the probability (p) of an upward price move?
explanation: Risk-neutral pricing: 50 = (60p + 40(1−p)) / 1.05. Solving: 52.5 = 60p + 40 − 40p → 20p = 12.5 → p = 0.625. This is the direct risk-neutral example from ICAI Section 7.2.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Ch.9 — Section 7.2 Risk Neutral Method Example

stem: Current share price of Penchant Ltd. = ₹420. In 3 months, maximum price = ₹500 and minimum = ₹400. Risk-free rate = 8% p.a. continuously compounded. Strike price = ₹450. Given e^0.02 = 1.0202. What is the value of the 3-month call option using the Risk Neutral method?
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Ch.9 — Illustration 2 & Practical Q.24 (Penchant Ltd.)

stem: Mr. A buys a 3-month call option (premium ₹30, strike ₹550) and a 3-month put option (premium ₹5, strike ₹450) on XYZ Ltd. for 100 shares each. The market price falls to ₹350 at expiry. What is his net profit or loss?
option_a: Profit of ₹10,000
option_b: Loss of ₹3,500
option_c: Loss of ₹500
option_d: Profit of ₹6,500
explanation: At ₹350: Call is out-of-the-money (not exercised). Put is exercised: gross gain = (₹450 − ₹350) × 100 = ₹10,000. Total premium paid = (₹30 + ₹5) × 100 = ₹3,500. Net profit = ₹10,000 − ₹3,500 = ₹6,500.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Ch.9 — Practical Q.15 & Q.19 (Straddle Payoff)

stem: CAB Ltd. share is currently ₹30. u = 1.10, d = 0.90. Risk-free rate = 5% p.a. Each period = 6 months. Strike price = ₹28. Risk-neutral p (upward) = 0.6235. Terminal payoffs: C_uu = ₹8.30, C_ud = ₹1.70, C_dd = ₹0. What is the current fair value of the European call option using the two-period binomial model?
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Ch.9 — Illustration 1 (CAB Ltd., Two-Period Binomial Model)

stem: TIC Ltd. share = ₹415. Strike = ₹400. Risk-free rate = 5% p.a. Volatility = 22%. Time = 3 months. Given: d₁ = 0.5033, d₂ = 0.3933, N(d₁) = 0.6928, N(d₂) = 0.6529, e^0.0125 = 1.012578. What is the Black-Scholes call option value, and is it worth buying at a market premium of ₹25?
option_a: ₹35.50; yes, option is underpriced
option_b: ₹25.00; option is fairly priced
option_c: ₹22.10; no, option is overpriced
option_d: ₹29.60; yes, option is underpriced
explanation: C = S×N(d₁) − (X/e^rT)×N(d₂) = 415×0.6928 − (400/1.012578)×0.6529 = 287.512 − 395.03×0.6529 = 287.512 − 257.916 = ₹29.60. Since market price ₹25 < BSM value ₹29.60, the option is underpriced and worth buying.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Ch.9 — Illustration 3(i) (TIC Ltd., Black-Scholes)

stem: Using TIC Ltd. data with current price ₹380: call value = ₹10.52, e^0.0125 = 1.012578, Strike = ₹400, T = 3 months. What is the put option value using put-call parity?
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Ch.9 — Illustration 3(iii) (TIC Ltd., Put via Put-Call Parity)

stem: A portfolio: A Ltd. (3 lakh shares × ₹500, β=1.40), B Ltd. (4 lakh shares × ₹750, β=1.20), C Ltd. (2 lakh shares × ₹250, β=1.60). Portfolio value = ₹5,000 lakh. Target beta = 0.91. Nifty futures at 8,125; each Nifty point worth ₹200. How many Nifty contracts must be sold to reduce beta to 0.91?
option_a: 90 contracts
option_b: 120 contracts
option_c: 150 contracts
option_d: 100 contracts
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Ch.9 — Practical Q.12(4) (Portfolio Beta Reduction)

stem: Ram buys 10,000 shares of X Ltd. at ₹22 (beta 1.5) and short-sells 5,000 shares of A Ltd. at ₹40 (beta 2). He hedges using Nifty futures at ₹1,000 each. Next day: X Ltd. drops 2%, A Ltd. rises 3%, Nifty futures drop 1.5%. What is his overall profit or loss?
option_a: Profit of ₹4,750
option_b: Loss of ₹11,450
option_c: Profit of ₹11,450
option_d: Break-even
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Ch.9 — Practical Q.10 (Ram's Combined Hedge)

stem: IPL considers a new pesticide plant (PV without abandonment option = ₹100 crore). If market conditions are favourable PV rises 30%; if sluggish PV falls 40% (disposal value = ₹80 crore). Risk-free rate = 8%. What is the present value of the abandonment option?
option_a: ₹5.81 crore
option_b: ₹8.00 crore
option_c: ₹6.28 crore
option_d: ₹20.00 crore
explanation: Risk-neutral p: 8% = p×30% + (1−p)×(−40%) → 0.92p = 0.48 → p = 0.686. If favourable (₹130 crore > ₹80 crore disposal), do not abandon → payoff = 0. If sluggish (₹60 crore < ₹80 crore), abandon → payoff = ₹80−₹60 = ₹20 crore. Expected payoff = 0.686×0 + 0.314×20 = ₹6.28 crore. PV = ₹6.28/1.08 = ₹5.81 crore.
icai_reference: ICAI AFM Ch.9 — Illustration 5 (IPL Abandonment Real Option)

stem: Which of the following is the correct interpretation of the quotation 1$ = ₹80?
option_a: The dollar is the price currency
option_b: $1 is the base currency and ₹80 is the price currency
option_c: ₹1 can be exchanged for $80
option_d: ₹80 is the base currency
explanation: In a forex quotation, the first currency is the base currency (for which price is quoted) and the second is the price/other currency. Here, $1 is base and ₹80 is price — meaning $1 costs ₹80.
icai_reference: CA Ajay Agarwal Notes – Pg 117: Meaning of Exchange Rate Quotation

stem: A Direct Quote for India means:
option_a: Number of INR per 1 unit of foreign currency
option_b: Number of foreign currency units per 1 unit of INR
option_c: The exchange rate quoted by RBI only
option_d: Number of USD per 1 unit of GBP
explanation: A direct quote expresses the home currency (INR) per unit of foreign currency. E.g., 1$ = ₹80 is a direct quote for India.
icai_reference: CA Ajay Agarwal Notes – Pg 117: Direct Quote

stem: The Bid Rate in the forex market is best described as:
option_a: The mid-point between two quoted rates
option_b: The rate quoted by RBI for inter-bank transactions
option_c: The rate at which the dealer is willing to buy the base currency
option_d: The rate at which the dealer is willing to sell the base currency
explanation: Bid Rate is the rate at which the dealer BUYS the base currency — it is the lower of the two rates since dealers always buy cheap.
icai_reference: CA Ajay Agarwal Notes – Pg 119: Bid Rate and Offer (Ask) Rate

stem: If the quotation is 1$ = ₹80/81, then the spread is:
explanation: Spread = Offer Rate − Bid Rate = ₹81 − ₹80 = ₹1. Spread represents the dealer's profit margin.
icai_reference: CA Ajay Agarwal Notes – Pg 121: Spread

stem: Spot Rate in the forex market refers to:
option_a: Rate at which delivery is made on the same day (spot date)
option_b: Rate fixed by RBI for import transactions
option_c: Rate for currency futures contracts
option_d: Rate applicable for transactions to be settled after 30 days
explanation: Spot Rate is the rate at which delivery will be made on the spot date (same day/T+2). Forward rate involves a future delivery date.
icai_reference: CA Ajay Agarwal Notes – Pg 121: Spot Rate and Forward Rate

stem: In the forex market, who regulates foreign currency transactions in India?
option_a: IRDA
option_b: SEBI
option_c: Ministry of Finance
option_d: RBI
explanation: RBI (Reserve Bank of India) regulates all foreign currency transactions in India. Authorised dealers (banks) act under RBI's supervision.
icai_reference: CA Ajay Agarwal Notes – Pg 119: Process in Foreign Exchange Market

stem: Forward Rate is the rate at which:
option_a: Banks buy currency from other banks at present
option_b: Delivery will be made at a future date
option_c: RBI sells foreign currency to exporters
option_d: Delivery is made on the spot date
explanation: Forward Rate is the pre-agreed rate for a transaction where actual delivery (exchange of currencies) takes place at a specified future date.
icai_reference: CA Ajay Agarwal Notes – Pg 121: Spot Rate and Forward Rate

stem: Which currency has the HIGHEST value among the following in descending order as per convention?
option_a: JPY (¥)
option_b: GBP (£)
option_c: USD ($)
option_d: INR (₹)
explanation: In descending order of currency value: GBP (£) > USD ($) > INR (₹) > JPY (¥). GBP is the strongest among these.
icai_reference: CA Ajay Agarwal Notes – Pg 119: Currency Value Hierarchy

stem: Merchant Rate is the rate at which:
option_a: A dealer transacts with customers
option_b: Currency futures are priced on the exchange
option_c: RBI sets the daily reference rate
option_d: Interbank transactions occur between two dealers
explanation: Merchant Rate is the rate at which dealers transact with customers. It = Inter-Bank Rate ± Exchange Margin.
icai_reference: CA Ajay Agarwal Notes – Pg 122: Merchant Rate and Inter-Bank Rate

stem: A forward contract entered into as a hedging tool gives the customer:
option_a: The option to cancel without any charges
option_b: The right but not the obligation to exchange at the agreed rate
option_c: An obligation to exchange currency at the agreed forward rate
option_d: Access to the interbank rate directly
explanation: A forward contract is a binding obligation — both parties MUST exchange at the agreed rate on the maturity date. It is not an option.
icai_reference: CA Ajay Agarwal Notes – Pg 134: Forward Contract as Hedging Technique

stem: If 1$ = ₹80, how many rupees will an importer pay to purchase $500?
explanation: Since the base currency ($) is the same as the currency being purchased, Amount = $500 × ₹80 = ₹40,000.
icai_reference: CA Ajay Agarwal Notes – Pg 118: Conversion of Amount from One Currency to Another

stem: If 1$ = ₹80, what is the indirect quote for India?
explanation: Indirect quote = 1/Direct quote = 1/80 = 0.0125. So 1₹ = $0.0125 is the indirect quote for India.
icai_reference: CA Ajay Agarwal Notes – Pg 118: Direct and Indirect Quote

stem: Spot Rate: 1$ = ₹80/81. What is the spread?
explanation: Spread = Offer Rate − Bid Rate = ₹81 − ₹80 = ₹1.
icai_reference: CA Ajay Agarwal Notes – Pg 121: Spread = Offer Rate − Bid Rate

stem: Given: Spot Rate 1$ = ₹80; 3-Month Forward Premium (Bid) = ₹3. What is the 3-month Forward Bid Rate?
explanation: Forward Rate = Spot Rate + Premium = ₹80 + ₹3 = ₹83.
icai_reference: CA Ajay Agarwal Notes – Pg 121: Forward Rate = Spot Rate + Premium

stem: An Indian exporter will receive $10,000 after 3 months. Spot Rate: 1$ = ₹82. The exporter sells $ at spot to get approximately:
explanation: The exporter sells $ and receives INR. Amount = $10,000 × ₹82 = ₹8,20,000.
icai_reference: CA Ajay Agarwal Notes – Pg 118: Currency Conversion

stem: From the customer's perspective, when a customer wants to BUY the base currency (e.g., $), which rate is applied?
option_a: The forward rate irrespective of maturity
option_b: The mid-rate between Bid and Ask
option_c: The lower (Bid) rate
option_d: The higher (Offer/Ask) rate
explanation: Adverse rate applies to customers. When a customer buys base currency (e.g., $), the dealer sells it — at the higher Offer rate. Remember: dealer always benefits from the spread.
icai_reference: CA Ajay Agarwal Notes – Pg 120: Adverse Rate Applied to Customers

stem: If Forward Rate > Spot Rate for base currency, the base currency is said to be at:
option_a: Par
option_b: Equilibrium
option_c: Premium
option_d: Discount
explanation: If the forward rate is higher than the spot rate for the base currency, it is at a Premium (it costs more in the future). Conversely, if forward < spot, it is at a Discount.
icai_reference: CA Ajay Agarwal Notes – Pg 121: Premium and Discount

stem: Premium in the forward market is presented as (Bid Premium | Offer Premium). Which value is always lower?
option_a: It depends on the currency pair
option_b: Bid Premium
option_c: Both are equal
option_d: Offer Premium
explanation: Premium = First Low, Then High. So Bid Premium < Offer Premium always. E.g., ₹3/₹3.50 means Bid premium = ₹3, Offer premium = ₹3.50.
icai_reference: CA Ajay Agarwal Notes – Pg 121: Premium presented as First Low Then High

stem: When a Forward Rate is required for a broken period (period for which forward premium/discount is not given), the premium is calculated using:
option_a: Interpolation between the two surrounding periods
option_b: Simple proportion of annual rate
option_c: Interest Rate Parity formula only
option_d: The spot rate directly
explanation: For broken periods, interpolation is used: Premium for required days = ((Next Period Premium − Previous Period Premium) / 30) × Days beyond previous period.
icai_reference: CA Ajay Agarwal Notes – Pg 124: Broken Period Interpolation

stem: According to Interest Rate Parity (IRP), the currency of a country with a HIGHER interest rate will be at:
option_a: Premium if inflation is also high
option_b: Premium in the forward market
option_c: Par with the other currency
option_d: Discount in the forward market
explanation: Per IRP, higher interest rate currency is at Discount (it depreciates forward) and lower interest rate currency is at Premium. This prevents covered interest arbitrage.
icai_reference: CA Ajay Agarwal Notes – Pg 126/127: IRP – Higher Interest Rate = Discount

stem: The formula for Annual Premium/Discount for base currency using exchange rate differential is:
option_a: (Spot Rate − Forward Rate) / Forward Rate × 100 × 12/n
option_b: (Spot Rate − Forward Rate) / Spot Rate × 100 × 365/n
option_c: (Forward Rate − Spot Rate) / Spot Rate × 100 × 12/n
option_d: (Forward Rate − Spot Rate) / Forward Rate × 100 × 365/n
explanation: Annual Prem/Disc = (Forward Rate − Spot Rate) / Spot Rate × 100 × 365/n (or 12/n if n is in months). Positive = Premium; Negative = Discount.
icai_reference: CA Ajay Agarwal Notes – Pg 126: Method I – Using Exchange Rate Differential

stem: In Money Market Hedging for an importer (foreign currency liability), the first step at the spot date is to:
option_a: Sell the foreign currency forward
option_b: Buy currency options for the liability amount
option_c: Enter into a forward contract for the liability amount
option_d: Borrow home currency, convert to foreign currency at spot, and invest in the foreign country
explanation: For a foreign currency liability: Borrow HC → Convert to FC at Spot → Invest FC in foreign country → At maturity, withdraw FC investment to pay liability → Repay HC borrowing (actual outflow).
icai_reference: CA Ajay Agarwal Notes – Pg 136: Money Market Cover Process for FC Liability

stem: In the fate of a forward contract, if the customer defaults and does NOT come on the 3rd day after maturity, the contract is:
option_a: Delivered at the original contracted rate
option_b: Automatically cancelled by the dealer
option_c: Extended automatically by the dealer
option_d: Cancelled at the customer's request
explanation: As per FEDAI rules, if a customer does not come on the 3rd day after maturity, the contract is automatically cancelled by the dealer. Loss is charged; profit is NOT passed on to the customer.
icai_reference: CA Ajay Agarwal Notes – Pg 132: Automatic Cancellation on 3rd Day

stem: Leading payment (early payment) decision for an importer involves:
option_a: Paying late to benefit from currency depreciation of payable currency
option_b: Deferring payment and investing surplus funds
option_c: Cancelling the forward contract and paying at spot rate
option_d: Paying early by availing supplier discount and paying interest on borrowed funds
explanation: Leading = Early Payment. Importer avails discount (if any), converts at current rate, and pays interest on borrowed funds from leading date to lagging date (opportunity cost).
icai_reference: CA Ajay Agarwal Notes – Pg 135: Leading or Lagging Payment Decision

stem: Geographical Arbitrage (Two-Point Arbitrage) is possible when:
option_a: Interest rates differ between two countries
option_b: The same currency has different exchange rates in two different markets
option_c: Inflation rates differ between two countries
option_d: Forward rate differs from the expected spot rate
explanation: Geographical (Two-Point) Arbitrage exploits different exchange rates for the same currency pair in two markets. Buy low in one market, sell high in another for riskless profit.
icai_reference: CA Ajay Agarwal Notes – Pg 144-145: Geographical Arbitrage

stem: Transaction Exposure in forex arises from:
option_a: Consolidation of subsidiary financial statements
option_b: Change in demand for a product due to exchange rate movement
option_c: A forex transaction entered at spot but settled at a future date
option_d: Translation of foreign currency financial statements
explanation: Transaction Exposure = P&L due to a transaction entered at spot rate but settled at a future date when the rate has changed. It is a cash flow exposure.
icai_reference: CA Ajay Agarwal Notes – Pg 155: Transaction Exposure

stem: Spot Rate: 1$ = ₹80/80.50. Exchange Margin: 0.10%. An Indian importer needs to buy $. What is the rate applicable? (Round to 4 decimals)
option_c: ₹79.9200 (Bid – margin)
explanation: Importer buys $ → Dealer sells $ → Use Offer Rate (High) = ₹80.50. Add Exchange Margin @ 0.10% = 80.50 × 0.001 = ₹0.0805. Rate = ₹80.50 + ₹0.0805 = ₹80.5805.
icai_reference: CA Ajay Agarwal Notes – Pg 122: Merchant Rate Calculation (Spot Selling Rate for Customer)

stem: Spot: 1$ = ₹80/80.50; 2-Month Forward Premium: ₹0.55/₹0.73; Exchange Margin: 0.10%. Calculate the 2-Month Forward Buying Rate of $ for the dealer (i.e., 2M Forward Selling Rate of $ for the customer). (Round to 4 decimals)
explanation: Dealer buys $ forward → Customer sells $ → Use Spot Bid (Low) = ₹80. Add Premium (Low) = ₹0.55 → Forward = ₹80.55. Less Exchange Margin @ 0.10% = 80.55 × 0.001 = ₹0.0806 → Rate = ₹80.55 − ₹0.0806 = ₹80.4694.
icai_reference: CA Ajay Agarwal Notes – Pg 122-123: 2-Month Forward Buying Rate Calculation

stem: Spot Rate: 1$ = ₹80; 1M Premium = ₹0.25; 2M Premium = ₹0.30; 3M Premium = ₹0.50. Calculate the Forward Rate for 2 months 15 days.
explanation: Premium for 15 days after 2 months = (3M Premium − 2M Premium)/30 × 15 = (0.50−0.30)/30 × 15 = ₹0.10. Total premium = ₹0.30 + ₹0.10 = ₹0.40. Forward Rate = ₹80 + ₹0.40 = ₹80.40.
icai_reference: CA Ajay Agarwal Notes – Pg 124: Broken Period Interpolation Example

stem: Spot Rate: 1$ = ₹80; 1-Year Forward Rate: 1$ = ₹84. What is the annual forward premium on $ using the exchange rate differential method?
explanation: Annual Prem = (84−80)/80 × 100 × (1/1) = 4/80 × 100 = 5.00% p.a. This is premium on $ (base currency).
icai_reference: CA Ajay Agarwal Notes – Pg 126: Method I Example – Annual Premium on $

stem: Interest Rate: India = 10% p.a., USA = 4% p.a. Using IRP (interest rate differential method), what is the annual premium on $ (approximately)?
explanation: Annual Prem on $ = (Int_India − Int_USA)/(1 + Int_USA) = (0.10−0.04)/(1+0.04) = 0.06/1.04 = 5.77% p.a. (approximately).
icai_reference: CA Ajay Agarwal Notes – Pg 126-127: Method II – IRP Example

stem: Spot Rate 1$ = ₹80; Annual Premium on $ = 5%. Calculate the 3-month forward rate.
icai_reference: CA Ajay Agarwal Notes – Pg 127-128: Forward Rate from Annual Premium Example

stem: Markets: Market I: 1$ = ₹40.25/40.50; Market II: 1$ = ₹40.52/40.62. What is the arbitrage profit per $ in geographical two-point arbitrage?
explanation: Buy $ in Market I at ₹40.50 (Offer). Sell $ in Market II at ₹40.52 (Bid). Profit = ₹40.52 − ₹40.50 = ₹0.02 per $.
icai_reference: CA Ajay Agarwal Notes – Pg 145: Two-Point Arbitrage Example

stem: Spot Rate: 1$ = ₹70/70.50; Interest Rate: India = 10%/12%, USA = 4%/5%. An Indian importer needs to pay $1 lakh after 3 months. Under Money Market Hedge, what amount of $ does the importer deposit at spot to meet the $1 lakh liability? (Use lower US interest rate for deposit)
option_b: $1,00,000 exactly
option_c: $97,087 approx
option_d: $98,522 approx
explanation: MV = P(1+R). P = MV/(1+R) = $1,00,000/(1+0.04×3/12) = $1,00,000/1.01 = $99,009.90. The importer deposits this amount at spot so it grows to $1 lakh at maturity.
icai_reference: CA Ajay Agarwal Notes – Pg 136: Money Market Hedge Step 2 – Importer Example

stem: Spot Rate 1$ = ₹80; 1-Year Forward Rate 1$ = ₹45 (incorrect – meant India Int = 12%, USA Int = 4%, Spot = ₹40). Using IRP, what is the expected 1-year forward rate? (Spot 1$ = ₹40, India IR = 12%, USA IR = 4%)
explanation: F/S = (1+R_India)/(1+R_USA) = (1.12)/(1.04) = 1.0769. F = ₹40 × 1.0769 = ₹43.08 (approximately). Higher Indian interest rate → rupee depreciates ($ at premium).
icai_reference: CA Ajay Agarwal Notes – Pg 130: IRP Theory – Expected Forward Rate

stem: In the cancellation of a forward contract, which of the following correctly describes 'Exchange Difference' (Cancellation Gain/Loss)?
option_a: A flat fee charged by the dealer on cancellation
option_b: Profit or loss due to swap transactions in the interbank market
option_c: The difference between the initial transaction rate with customer and the interbank cancellation rate
option_d: Interest charged for late settlement of the cancelled contract
explanation: Exchange Difference = Difference between the rate originally contracted with the customer and the rate at which the dealer covers in the interbank market on the cancellation date. Per FEDAI, exchange margin is applied on the interbank cancellation transaction.
icai_reference: CA Ajay Agarwal Notes – Pg 133: Exchange Difference (Cancellation Gain/Loss)

stem: In automatic cancellation of a forward contract, which statement is TRUE?
option_a: Both profit and loss due to automatic cancellation are borne by the dealer
