option_b: Profit due to cancellation is passed to the customer; loss is also recovered
option_c: The contract is rolled over at the original rate
option_d: Loss is charged to the customer but profit due to automatic cancellation is NOT passed on
explanation: In automatic cancellation: Loss is charged from the customer but Profit is NOT given to the customer. This differs from regular cancellation where profit IS given to customer.
icai_reference: CA Ajay Agarwal Notes – Pg 133: Automatic Cancellation – Asymmetric Treatment

stem: In Covered Interest Arbitrage, the decision to borrow in a particular currency is driven by which comparison?
option_a: Spot rate vs forward rate only
option_b: Current account surplus vs deficit
option_c: Inflation rate differential vs exchange rate differential
option_d: Interest rate differential vs exchange rate differential (Annual Premium/Discount)
explanation: CIA compares: (1) Interest Rate Differential and (2) Exchange Rate Differential (Annual Prem/Disc). Borrow in currency where the higher differential gives an advantage. If Exchange Rate Differential is higher, borrow in the high-interest rate currency.
icai_reference: CA Ajay Agarwal Notes – Pg 146: Covered Interest Arbitrage Decision

stem: Translation Exposure (Accounting Exposure) in foreign exchange is best described as:
option_a: Loss due to early cancellation of a forward contract
option_b: Change in demand for products due to exchange rate movements
option_c: Profit or loss due to translation of foreign currency assets/liabilities in financial statements
option_d: Profit or loss on transactions entered in foreign currency but settled later
explanation: Translation Exposure = P&L due to translating foreign currency-denominated assets/liabilities into home currency for financial statement preparation. It is a balance sheet exposure.
icai_reference: CA Ajay Agarwal Notes – Pg 155: Translation Exposure (Accounting Exposure)

stem: In International Cash Management, the 'Immediate Cash Pooling Method' involves:
option_a: Netting inter-company receivables and payables only
option_b: Converting all subsidiaries' surplus cash into the parent's home currency at spot rate and investing the total pool
option_c: Subsidiaries borrowing centrally and remitting to the parent
option_d: Each subsidiary investing its surplus in its own currency and converting at forward rate
explanation: Immediate Cash Pooling: Convert surplus of ALL subsidiaries to parent's home currency at SPOT rate → Pool the total → Invest total in parent's home currency. Compare with Acting Independently method to determine the better approach.
icai_reference: CA Ajay Agarwal Notes – Pg 150: International Cash Management – Immediate Cash Pooling Method

stem: A currency swap differs from an interest rate swap in that a currency swap involves:
option_a: Exchange of principal and interest in one currency for principal and interest in another currency
option_b: Only the exchange of interest rate differentials between two parties
option_c: Exchange of credit risk between two counterparties
option_d: Exchange of fixed for floating interest only, with no exchange of principal
explanation: A currency swap involves exchange of BOTH principal and interest in one currency for principal and interest in another. Both principal exchanges happen (at start and maturity) unlike interest rate swaps where principal is notional.
icai_reference: CA Ajay Agarwal Notes – Pg 155: Currency Swap

stem: Spot Rate: 1$ = ₹40; 1-Year Forward Rate: 1$ = ₹45; India IR = 12% p.a.; USA IR = 4% p.a. Arbitrageur borrows ₹40 lakhs. What is the arbitrage gain at the end of 1 year? (Assume borrow ₹ → convert to $ at spot → deposit in USA → convert back at forward → repay ₹ loan)
option_c: No gain — covered arbitrage is not profitable
explanation: Borrow ₹40L at 12% → repay ₹44.8L. Convert ₹40L to $ at spot: $1L. Deposit in USA at 4% → $1.04L at maturity. Convert at forward: $1.04L × ₹45 = ₹46.8L. Gain = ₹46.8L − ₹44.8L = ₹2,00,000.
icai_reference: CA Ajay Agarwal Notes – Pg 146-147: Covered Interest Arbitrage Full Numerical

stem: Spot Rate: 1$ = ₹70/70.50; India IR = 10%/12%; USA IR = 4%/5%. An Indian exporter will receive $1 lakh after 3 months. Under Money Market Hedge, what is the actual INR inflow at maturity?
explanation: Step1: $1L to receive. Step2: Borrow $ now = $1L/(1+0.05×3/12) = $98,765.43. Step3: Convert to ₹ at spot bid = $98,765.43 × ₹70 = ₹69,13,580. Step4: Invest in India at 10% for 3M = ₹69,13,580 × (1+0.025) = ₹70,86,420 approx.
icai_reference: CA Ajay Agarwal Notes – Pg 137-138: Money Market Hedge – Exporter Example Full Calculation

stem: Cross Rate: 1$ = ₹45.90; 1£ = 1.7850$; 1£ = SGD 3.1575. What is the cross rate for 1 SGD = ₹?
option_a: ₹81.95 approx
option_b: ₹25.94 approx
option_c: ₹20.50 approx
option_d: ₹14.53 approx
icai_reference: CA Ajay Agarwal Notes – Pg 149: Cross Rates – Example 4 (SGD)

stem: Party A borrows $10M at 5% p.a. and Party B borrows ₹80 crore at 9% p.a. They enter a currency swap. After 1 year, Party A pays ₹7.2 crore interest and receives $0.5M interest from Party B. Party A's net interest cost on the $10M borrowing after swap benefit, if ₹/$= ₹80, is:
explanation: Party A: Pays $0.5M to US lender. Receives $0.5M from Party B (net zero on $ interest). Pays ₹7.2Cr to Party B. ₹7.2Cr in $ terms = ₹7.2Cr/80 = $0.9M. Net cost = $0.9M on $10M borrowing; effective cost concept. Simplified illustrative answer = 4%.
icai_reference: CA Ajay Agarwal Notes – Pg 155-156: Currency Swap Steps

stem: Spot: 1$ = ₹80.80/80.81; 1M Forward = ₹0.10/0.11; 2M Forward = ₹0.21/0.22; 3M Forward = ₹0.32/0.33. Transit period = 20 days. A customer submits a bill for 60 days for selling $. Which forward rate (buying rate of $ for dealer) applies?
option_a: Lower of 2-Month and 3-Month Forward Bid Rate
option_b: 2-Month Forward Bid Rate (₹81.01)
option_c: Spot Bid Rate (₹80.80)
option_d: 3-Month Forward Bid Rate (₹81.12)
explanation: Bill tenor = 60 days. Add transit period 20 days → total = 80 days ≈ between 2M and 3M. Per the transit period rule, dealer buying $ → wants low rate → 2M Bid (₹80.80 + ₹0.21 = ₹81.01). Lower month rate applies.
icai_reference: CA Ajay Agarwal Notes – Pg 125: Transit Period – Rate of Lower or Upper Month Beneficial to Dealer

stem: An Indian company needs to pay £5 lakh in 3 months. Spot: 1£ = $1.60 = ₹128. 3M Forward: 1£ = $1.62 = ₹130. India IR = 12% p.a., UK IR = 5% p.a. Under forward cover, the total INR outflow for £5 lakh is:
explanation: Under forward contract, the company buys £ forward at ₹130 per £. Total outflow = £5,00,000 × ₹130 = ₹65,00,000.
icai_reference: CA Ajay Agarwal Notes – Pg 134: Forward Contract Hedging – Outflow Calculation

stem: Multinational Co. has two subsidiaries. Sub A (India) has surplus of ₹80 lakh; Sub B (USA) has surplus of $1 lakh. Spot: 1$=₹80. Forward: 1$=₹82. India IR=10% p.a., USA IR=4% p.a. Tenor = 1 year. Under Immediate Cash Pooling method, total INR available at maturity is approximately:
explanation: Immediate Cash Pooling: Convert all to ₹ at Spot = ₹80L + ($1L × ₹80) = ₹80L + ₹80L = ₹1,60,00,000. Invest pool in India at 10% for 1Y = ₹1,60,00,000 × 1.10 = ₹1,76,00,000.
icai_reference: CA Ajay Agarwal Notes – Pg 150: International Cash Management – Immediate Cash Pooling
